prompt: Riscrivi al passivo, al presente: 'Spediscono la lettera ogni giorno.' -> 'La lettera ____ ogni giorno.'
explanation: 'La lettera è spedita (o viene spedita) ogni giorno' means 'The letter is sent every day.' The passive turns the object into the subject: Italian builds it from essere (or the more dynamic venire) plus the past participle, which agrees with the new subject like an adjective. Lettera is feminine singular, so the participle takes -a: spedita. Working: è / viene spedita.
examiner_note: Two markpoints per criterion 8: voice (essere-not-avere) and agreement are separate skills. Both è and viene take full credit (simple tense, no forcing context).

prompt: Riscrivi al passivo, al presente: 'Il cliente paga il conto.' -> 'Il conto ____ dal cliente.'
explanation: 'Il conto è pagato (o viene pagato) dal cliente' means 'The bill is paid by the customer.' The affected thing (il conto) becomes the subject and the verb becomes essere, or the more dynamic venire, plus the past participle. Conto is masculine singular, so the participle stays -o: pagato. The doer returns after da: dal cliente. Working: è / viene pagato.
examiner_note: Single markpoint: masculine-singular agreement is the default -o, so agreement is not separately tested here; the point is essere/venire over avere. Breadcrumb only restates the named task (passivo), so left visible.

prompt: Riscrivi al passivo, al presente: 'Puliscono le stanze ogni mattina.' -> 'Le stanze ____ ogni mattina.'
explanation: 'Le stanze sono pulite (o vengono pulite) ogni mattina' means 'The rooms are cleaned every morning.' In the passive the subject is now le stanze, feminine plural, and the participle agrees with it like an adjective, taking -e: pulite. The finite verb is the plural essere (sono) or venire (vengono). Working: sono / vengono pulite.
examiner_note: Voice markpoint accepts sono and vengono (both full credit). Agreement markpoint catches the masculine-default puliti and the singular forms.

prompt: Riscrivi al passivo, al passato prossimo: 'A Napoli hanno inventato la pizza.' -> 'La pizza ____ a Napoli.'
explanation: 'La pizza è stata inventata a Napoli' means 'Pizza was invented in Naples.' In a compound tense the passive stacks two participles: essere itself becomes stato/stata, then the main participle follows, and both agree with the subject. Pizza is feminine singular, so both go to -a: è stata inventata. The avere of the active version disappears. Working: è stata inventata.
examiner_note: Voice markpoint is the bare auxiliary è (present of essere), agreement markpoint is the whole participle stack so both stata and inventata must be feminine. must_not_include catches every mixed-gender combination.

prompt: Riscrivi al passivo, al passato prossimo: 'Hanno costruito le chiese nel Medioevo.' -> 'Le chiese ____ nel Medioevo.'
explanation: 'Le chiese sono state costruite nel Medioevo' means 'The churches were built in the Middle Ages.' The compound passive stacks essere's own participle (state) before the main one (costruite), and both agree with the feminine-plural subject in -e. The finite auxiliary is the plural sono. Working: sono state costruite.

prompt: Riscrivi al passivo, al futuro: 'Costruiranno il ponte l'anno prossimo.' -> 'Il ponte ____ l'anno prossimo.'
explanation: 'Il ponte sarà costruito (o verrà costruito) l'anno prossimo' means 'The bridge will be built next year.' The passive can sit in any tense: put essere (or venire) into the future and add the participle. Ponte is masculine singular, so the participle stays -o: sarà / verrà costruito. Working: sarà costruito.
examiner_note: Tense pinned to future by 'l'anno prossimo' (criterion 10a); must_not_include catches the present 'è costruito' (wrong tense) and the avere future 'avrà'. Both sarà and verrà credited.

prompt: Riscrivi al passivo, all'imperfetto: 'Ogni domenica celebravano la messa in latino.' -> 'Ogni domenica la messa ____ in latino.'
explanation: 'Ogni domenica la messa era celebrata (o veniva celebrata) in latino' means 'Every Sunday mass was celebrated in Latin.' A repeated past action wants the imperfect, so the auxiliary goes to the imperfect: era or the dynamic veniva. Messa is feminine singular, so the participle is celebrata. Working: era / veniva celebrata.
examiner_note: Habitual 'ogni domenica' pins the imperfect. veniva is idiomatic for a recurring process and is credited alongside era.

prompt: Riscrivi al passivo, al passato prossimo: 'Ieri hanno firmato i documenti.' -> 'Ieri i documenti ____.'
explanation: 'Ieri i documenti sono stati firmati' means 'Yesterday the documents were signed.' In the compound passive both participles agree with the subject: documenti is masculine plural, so essere's participle and the main participle both take -i, stati firmati, after the plural auxiliary sono. Working: sono stati firmati.

prompt: Riscrivi al passivo, al presente: 'Fanno il pane ogni mattina.' -> 'Il pane ____ ogni mattina.'
explanation: 'Il pane viene fatto ogni mattina' means 'Bread is made every morning.' In a simple tense the passive auxiliary can be venire instead of essere, and venire adds a sense of an action happening rather than a state: viene fatto (is made, gets made) over è fatto. Pane is masculine singular, so the participle stays -o. Working: viene / è fatto.
examiner_note: A recurring-process frame slightly favours venire; per the dispatch ruling è fatto is not penalised and takes full credit.

prompt: Riscrivi al passivo, al passato prossimo: 'Ieri hanno arrestato il ladro.' -> 'Ieri il ladro ____.'

any_phrases.note: Correct, and it rightly avoids the impossible venire compound, but the item asked for the passato prossimo: è stato arrestato.

explanation: 'Ieri il ladro è stato arrestato' means 'Yesterday the thief was arrested.' Venire only works as the passive auxiliary in simple tenses. In a compound tense like the passato prossimo it is impossible: there is no 'è venuto arrestato'. You fall back to essere: è stato arrestato. Working: è stato arrestato.
examiner_note: Tests the venire compound-tense restriction; the correct answer uses essere. Breadcrumb left visible because naming venire tempts exactly the catchable 'è venuto arrestato' error (leak-vs-trap, criterion 15). The passato remoto 'fu arrestato' scores 0.9 under the Rev 17 dodge rule: correct passive, but not the requested tense.

prompt: Riscrivi al passivo, al passato remoto (stile narrativo): 'Chiusero la porta alle otto.' -> 'La porta ____ alle otto.'
explanation: 'La porta venne chiusa (o fu chiusa) alle otto' means 'The door was closed at eight.' The passato remoto is a simple tense, so venire is available: venne chiusa, a shade more dynamic than the essere version fu chiusa. Porta is feminine singular, so the participle is chiusa. Working: venne / fu chiusa.
examiner_note: Register cued as narrative (criterion 11) to license the passato remoto. Both venne and fu credited. must_not_include's bare 'è' catches a present-tense slip.

prompt: Completa con la forma dinamica (l'azione che si ripete, non lo stato): 'Il negozio ____ alle venti ogni sera.' (chiudere)

markpoints.label: Venire for the action, not the state

explanation: 'Il negozio viene chiuso alle venti ogni sera' means 'The shop is closed (gets closed) at eight every evening.' Here the meaning is a repeated action, someone closing the shop, not the resulting state. Venire captures the action (viene chiuso); è chiuso would describe the state of being shut, a different meaning. Working: viene chiuso.
examiner_note: The prompt cues the dynamic-action reading in plain words (not the rule), so 'è chiuso' (the state) is a meaning miss, not a dodge, and stays in must_not_include. candidate_forms/correct_form added per Rev 17 (iii), which generalises criterion 16 to non-tense discriminations; flagged for ratification since this leaf is a formation leaf, not a '.discrimination' bucket, so the criterion's trigger does not strictly fire.

prompt: Riscrivi al passivo, al presente: 'Pagano le tasse ogni anno.' -> 'Le tasse ____ ogni anno.'
explanation: 'Le tasse vengono pagate (o sono pagate) ogni anno' means 'Taxes are paid every year.' The recurring-process sense makes venire natural, and the plural finite form is vengono. Tasse is feminine plural, so the participle is pagate. The essere version sono pagate is equally correct. Working: vengono / sono pagate.

prompt: 'Questo modulo va compilato a mano' vuol dire: 'This form ____ filled in by hand.'
explanation: 'Questo modulo va compilato a mano' means 'This form must be filled in by hand.' Andare followed by a past participle does not mean physical motion here: it expresses obligation, that something must or has to be done. So va compilato = must be filled in, not 'goes filled'. The reading to avoid is treating va as 'goes'.
examiner_note: Recognition item (it->en): the answer is the English obligation phrasing. must_not_include catches the literal-motion misreading. Breadcrumb suppressed since the label states the answer (obligation).

prompt: Completa esprimendo obbligo (meaning: they must be taken): 'Le medicine ____ a stomaco pieno.' (prendere, con andare)

any_phrases.note: Correct Italian, but it sidesteps the andare obligation construction: vanno prese is the idiomatic form.

explanation: 'Le medicine vanno prese a stomaco pieno' means 'The medicines must be taken on a full stomach.' Andare plus a participle expresses obligation, so vanno prese = must be taken. It agrees like any passive: medicine is feminine plural, giving prese, after the plural vanno. Working: vanno prese.
examiner_note: Criterion 20 (Rev 22): the earlier Italian gloss '(l'idea: devono essere prese)' handed over BOTH the agreement answer 'prese' (markpoint 1's whole test) and the 0.9 dodge phrase verbatim, so a learner could copy the cue for 1.9/2 without reaching for andare. Recued by bracketed English meaning; the citation-form trigger '(prendere, con andare)' is exempt and stays, so prompt_supplies_base_form is true. Rev 17 0.9-for-dodges: 'devono essere prese' stays a 0.9 dodge with a steering note, now a genuine sidestep rather than a copy of the cue. Two markpoints: obligation-auxiliary and agreement.

prompt: Completa esprimendo obbligo con andare: 'Il modulo ____ in stampatello.' (compilare, con andare)

any_phrases.note: Correct Italian, but it sidesteps the construction this item drills: for instructions the idiomatic form is va compilato.

explanation: 'Il modulo va compilato in stampatello' means 'The form must be filled in in block capitals.' Andare plus the participle carries the sense of necessity: va compilato = must be filled in. Modulo is masculine singular, so the participle stays -o. Working: va compilato.
examiner_note: Rev 17 0.9-for-dodges: the periphrastic 'deve essere compilato' is correct Italian that sidesteps the andare pattern, so it scores 0.9 with a steering note rather than being caught as wrong (it was in must_not_include at Rev 14, which would have told a learner good Italian was an error). must_not_include still catches the state reading 'è compilato'.

prompt: Completa con l'espressione di perdita: 'Il pacco ____ durante il trasloco.' (perdere)
explanation: 'Il pacco è andato perso durante il trasloco' means 'The parcel got lost during the move.' With a small set of verbs (perdere, smarrire) andare plus the participle does not mean obligation but loss: andare perso = to get lost. It behaves like a real motion verb here, taking essere in the compound and agreeing (pacco masculine, andato perso). Working: è andato perso.
examiner_note: This is the lexical loss sense of andare + participle, distinct from the obligation sense. must_not_include catches the plain state 'è perso' and the avere version. See bucket_suggestions: the andare leaf conflates obligation and loss.

prompt: 'Questa lettera va spedita subito' vuol dire: 'This letter ____ sent immediately.'
explanation: 'Questa lettera va spedita subito' means 'This letter must be sent immediately.' Again andare plus a participle is obligation, not motion: va spedita = must be sent, not 'goes sent'. Reading va as 'goes' is the trap this item guards against.
examiner_note: Second recognition angle (it->en) with a different verb; must_not_include catches both the 'goes' motion reading and a mistaken progressive 'is being'.

prompt: Completa con la preposizione dell'agente: 'Il ritratto è stato dipinto ____ Caravaggio.'
explanation: 'Il ritratto è stato dipinto da Caravaggio' means 'The portrait was painted by Caravaggio.' When the passive still names who did the action, that doer is the agent, and Italian introduces it with da, never di (which would sound possessive, 'of Caravaggio') and never per. Working: da Caravaggio.
examiner_note: 'da' word-anchored so it does not substring-match inside dal/dalla. must_not_include catches the di calque and the per calque (from English 'by' via Spanish por). Breadcrumb suppressed: the label names da, which is the answer.

prompt: Completa con la preposizione articolata dell'agente: 'La decisione è stata presa ____ direttore.'
explanation: 'La decisione è stata presa dal direttore' means 'The decision was taken by the director.' When the agent has an article, da fuses with it: da + il = dal. The trap is del (di + il), which reads as 'of the director', a possessive, not the doer. Working: dal direttore.
examiner_note: Tests the da + article contraction. must_not_include catches del (the di calque) and dallo (wrong contraction before a plain consonant).

prompt: Completa con la preposizione articolata dell'agente: 'Il progetto sarà approvato ____ commissione.'
explanation: 'Il progetto sarà approvato dalla commissione' means 'The project will be approved by the committee.' Da fuses with a feminine article too: da + la = dalla. The possessive-looking della (di + la) is the miss to avoid; it would mean 'of the committee'. Working: dalla commissione.
examiner_note: Feminine contraction. must_not_include catches della (di calque) and the per calque.

prompt: Completa con la preposizione dell'agente: 'I fiori sono stati mandati ____ Marco.'
explanation: 'I fiori sono stati mandati da Marco' means 'The flowers were sent by Marco.' The agent is introduced by da. English 'by' tempts per (reinforced by Spanish por), but per would mean 'for Marco' or 'through Marco', not the sender. Working: da Marco.
examiner_note: Foregrounds the per calque as the primary trap; 'da' word-anchored. Lower band (A2) since the agent is a bare name with no contraction.

prompt: Completa con la preposizione articolata dell'agente: 'La legge è stata approvata ____ deputati.'
explanation: 'La legge è stata approvata dai deputati' means 'The law was passed by the members of parliament.' Before a masculine plural, da + i = dai (written as one word, not da i). The possessive dei (di + i) is the miss to avoid. Working: dai deputati.
examiner_note: Plural masculine contraction. must_not_include catches dei (di calque), the per calque, and the un-fused 'da i'.

prompt: Which is more natural in a television news bulletin?
choices: Il presidente è stato eletto ieri.
explanation: 'Il presidente è stato eletto ieri' means 'The president was elected yesterday.' News writing puts the affected thing first and leaves the doer out, which is exactly what the passive is for: the story is about the president, not about whoever counted the votes. The active 'Hanno eletto il presidente' is perfectly correct Italian, but its vague 'they' belongs in speech, not in a bulletin.
examiner_note: MCQ, index-scored: the register judgement is not reachable by the substring marker. Paired with pas_use_reg_02, which offers the identical two sentences and takes the opposite answer (house technique 3).

prompt: Which is more natural when you are telling a friend the news over coffee?
choices: Il presidente è stato eletto ieri.
explanation: 'Hanno eletto il presidente ieri' means 'They elected the president yesterday.' In everyday speech Italian reaches for the active with a vague 'they' rather than the passive. The passive version is not wrong, but it sounds like you are reading the news aloud. This is the commonest English-speaker habit to unlearn: English uses the passive far more freely than Italian does.
examiner_note: Same two sentences as pas_use_reg_01, opposite answer, per the Rev 14 house technique on same-surface pairs: the pair teaches the register conditioning better than either item alone.

prompt: Riscrivi come lo diresti a un amico al bar (parlato quotidiano): 'Il presidente è stato eletto ieri.' -> '____ il presidente ieri.'
explanation: 'Hanno eletto il presidente ieri' means 'They elected the president yesterday.' Spoken Italian prefers the active with an unspecified 'they' where English might keep the passive. Keeping 'è stato eletto' at the bar is the classic calque: grammatical, but it lands like a news anchor. Working: hanno eletto.
examiner_note: Register cued in plain words (criterion 11), and the voice is never named, so the choice stays the test. Rev 20 (i): keeping the passive here IS this leaf's named common_miss (passive overuse calqued from English), so it takes a flat WRONG and does not inherit the 0.9 dodge kindness.

prompt: Riscrivi per un avviso ufficiale in aeroporto (registro formale), mettendo il volo in primo piano: 'Hanno cancellato il volo delle 18.' -> '____'
explanation: 'Il volo delle 18 è stato cancellato' means 'The 18:00 flight has been cancelled.' An official notice keeps the doer out of sight and puts the affected thing first, so the passive is the register's default. Working: il volo delle 18 è stato cancellato.
examiner_note: Both the register and the information structure are pinned by the prompt (formal notice, flight first), so the active defies an explicit instruction rather than merely sidestepping a pattern: flat WRONG, not a Rev 17 (iv) dodge. Naming the doer is impossible here anyway.

prompt: Riscrivi per il parlato quotidiano: 'Il pane è fatto qui ogni giorno.' -> 'Qui ____ il pane ogni giorno.'
explanation: 'Qui si fa il pane ogni giorno' means 'They make bread here every day / Bread is made here every day.' For an everyday statement about what generally happens, Italian reaches for the si construction rather than the full passive: si fa il pane is what you would actually hear. The passive è fatto is grammatical but stiff in speech. Working: si fa.
examiner_note: Rev 20 (i): keeping the passive here is this leaf's named common_miss (passive overuse where Italian would use si or the active), so it is a flat WRONG and does not collect the 0.9 dodge kindness. candidate_forms per Rev 17 (iii): a genuine two-candidate form choice.

prompt: In a company's annual report, stating what happens each year (not an obligation), which is most idiomatic?
choices: Il bilancio è presentato ogni anno ai finanziatori.
Si presenta il bilancio ogni anno ai finanziatori.
Va presentato il bilancio ogni anno ai finanziatori.
explanation: 'Il bilancio è presentato ogni anno ai finanziatori' means 'The balance sheet is presented to the investors every year.' A formal report wants the passive: the document is the topic and the doer is irrelevant. 'Si presenta il bilancio' says the same thing but sounds spoken, and 'Va presentato il bilancio' changes the meaning altogether, since andare plus a participle expresses obligation (it must be presented).
examiner_note: From the OIC idea bank's triple rendering of one sentence. The prompt pins the meaning ('stating what happens, not an obligation') so the andare option is a genuine meaning distractor rather than an ambiguity: without that pin the item would be unfair, since va presentato is impeccable Italian for a different proposition.

prompt: Riscrivi per un titolo di giornale (registro formale, notizia): 'La polizia ha arrestato due uomini.' -> '____'
explanation: 'Due uomini sono stati arrestati dalla polizia' means 'Two men have been arrested by the police.' A headline foregrounds what happened to the people involved, so the passive is idiomatic, with the doer surviving after da if it is worth naming. Working: due uomini sono stati arrestati (dalla polizia).
examiner_note: The agent is optional in a correct answer, so the markpoint tests only the voice choice; the da-phrase has its own leaf and is not double-marked here (criterion 8).

prompt: Completa: 'I libri ____ dalla libreria Feltrinelli.' (vendere)

markpoints.label: Named agent forces the full passive

explanation: 'I libri sono venduti dalla libreria Feltrinelli' means 'The books are sold by the Feltrinelli bookshop.' The si construction and the full passive overlap almost everywhere, but they split on one test: si can never name the doer. Once a da-phrase appears, only the full passive works, so 'si vendono libri dalla libreria' is impossible. Working: sono venduti.
examiner_note: Rev 20 (i): the si form with a da-agent IS this leaf's named common_miss, so it takes a flat WRONG and does not inherit the 0.9 dodge kindness. Suppressed under the Rev 20 (ii) frame-forced class: the da-phrase in the sentence admits exactly one of the two candidates, so the set is recoverable pre-answer without the breadcrumb naming it.

prompt: Su un cartello in vetrina: 'Qui ____ libri usati.' (vendere)

any_phrases.note: Correct, but signage reaches for the lighter si form: qui si vendono libri usati.
Very widely said and heard, but non-standard: with a plural noun the verb agrees, si vendono libri.

explanation: 'Qui si vendono libri usati' means 'Second-hand books sold here.' With no doer to name, Italian prefers the lighter si construction, which is why shop windows are full of it. The verb still agrees with the thing: libri is plural, so si vendono, not si vende. Working: si vendono.
examiner_note: Three graded entries. The full passive is correct Italian sidestepping the drilled si form and is NOT this leaf's named common_miss (that is the agent-bolted-on error), so it keeps the Rev 17 (iv) 0.9. 'si vende' + plural noun takes 0.5 with a register note, for parity with the ruling on the si_constructions batch (Architecture_SiConstructionsAuthor_graded_credit_parity, RULED v2: the a-me-mi-piace class). Order matters: the engine returns the first matching entry, and no entry is a substring of another.

prompt: Which of these is NOT possible in Italian?
choices: I libri sono venduti da Marco.
explanation: All three sentences try to say 'Books are sold by Marco.' The impossible one is 'Si vendono libri da Marco'. The si construction hides the doer by design, so it cannot then name one: bolt a da-phrase on and the sentence breaks. To name Marco you need either the full passive ('I libri sono venduti da Marco') or the plain active ('Marco vende libri'). This is the single sharpest test for telling the two constructions apart.
examiner_note: MCQ, index-scored: 'pick the impossible one' cannot be marked by substring. This is the leaf's hot spot, the agent test, stated directly.

prompt: Su un cartello: 'Qui ____ camere.' (affittare)

markpoints.label: si passivante agreeing with a plural noun

any_phrases.note: Correct, but a sign reaches for the lighter si form: qui si affittano camere.
Very widely said and heard, but non-standard: camere is plural, so si affittano.

explanation: 'Qui si affittano camere' means 'Rooms to let here.' The si passivante agrees with the thing, not with an invisible doer: camere is plural, so the verb goes plural too. It is the same number instinct as c'è versus ci sono, and it is missed the same way. Working: si affittano.
examiner_note: Two markpoints (criterion 8). mp0 is the construction choice; mp1 is the number instinct, which BOTH constructions share (si affittano camere / camere sono affittate), so it accepts either plural form and guards the singular slips. That matters: without it the 0.9 passive dodge would also forfeit the agreement mark and score 0.9/2 for a correct answer. The 0.5 on 'si affitta' matches the ratified si-parity ruling (Architecture_SiConstructionsAuthor_graded_credit_parity v2), and decomposes correctly: 0.5 on the choice, WRONG on agreement, i.e. right construction, slipped on the number.

prompt: 'Qui si vendono libri usati' vuol dire: 'Second-hand books ____ here.'

markpoints.label: Reading si passivante as an English passive

explanation: 'Qui si vendono libri usati' means 'Second-hand books are sold here.' The si passivante has no English twin, so it lands in English as a plain passive. Reading it word by word as 'one sells' or 'it sells itself' is the trap: si here is not a real doer and not reflexive, it is the marker that lets Italian skip the doer entirely.
examiner_note: Recognition item (it->en), English answer, cleared to ship by Housing (thread Architecture_Housing_english_answer_grammar_items, CLOSED: norm() is language-agnostic, the accent rewriter is inert on English). Guards catch the two literal misreadings.

prompt: Riscrivi nominando chi vende: 'Qui si vendono libri usati.' -> 'I libri usati ____ da questa libreria.'

markpoints.label: Naming the agent forces the passive

explanation: 'I libri usati sono venduti da questa libreria' means 'The second-hand books are sold by this bookshop.' The moment you decide to name the seller, the si construction is no longer available: si exists precisely to leave the doer out. You switch to the full passive and the doer returns after da. Working: sono venduti.
examiner_note: The mirror of pas_use_vsi_01: there the agent was already in the frame, here the learner adds it and must notice that doing so rules the si form out. Keeping si is the named common_miss: flat WRONG.

prompt: Which is more natural on a shop window?
choices: Qui i libri usati sono venduti.
explanation: 'Qui si vendono libri usati' means 'Second-hand books sold here.' With nobody to name, the si construction is lighter and is what signage actually uses. The full passive 'i libri usati sono venduti' is grammatical but reads like a report about the shop rather than a sign in its window.
examiner_note: Pairs with pas_use_vsi_01 across the register axis: same two constructions, opposite winners, the conditioning factor being whether a doer is named. Index-scored.
